ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for lobster ball; closest defensible match is the generic seafood ball food product class. Lobster-specific ball product is not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans and products thereof — processed crustacean product
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers processed crustacean products; no specific lobster ball sub-code exists. Used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for lobster ball as a distinct food concept; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of lobster balls is approximately 100 g, equivalent to 5–6 individual balls, commonly served in hotpot, soup noodles, or as a side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Lobster Ball — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database (cr_id G00010749)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Lobster Ball (cr_id G00010749), an approved primary regional source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Saturated fat (0.21 g), monounsaturated fat (0.16 g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.27 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for spiny lobster (NDB 15147), an approved secondary global source, proportionally scaled to the reported total fat of 0.95 g per 100 g; HPB does not separately report fatty acid sub-fractions for this item. This adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — GI not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate seafood products
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Lobster Ball contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010749). Per the University of Sydney GI Database methodology and the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care), glycemic index testing requires a minimum available carbohydrate content; foods with negligible carbohydrates are classified as GI not applicable. No GI value is assigned. Glycemic load per 100 g serving is 0.0 by definition. This determination is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classification practice for protein- and fat-dominant foods.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 535 mg per 100 g in a processed seafood product
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit lobster ball intake to small portions (≤50 g) and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as soy sauce or chilli sauce, as the 535 mg sodium per 100 g already represents approximately 27% of the 2000 mg daily sodium target; actual sodium may be higher due to processing binders and seasoning.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.0 g per 100 g) and high protein (20.91 g per 100 g) support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lobster ball is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate content and high protein density, which supports satiety and stable postprandial blood glucose; however, starch binders may contribute trace carbohydrates and sweet dipping sauces should be avoided to prevent glycaemic load elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol (161 mg per 100 g) and high sodium (535 mg per 100 g) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate lobster ball consumption due to its high dietary cholesterol (161 mg per 100 g) and elevated sodium (535 mg per 100 g); the low saturated fat content (0.21 g per 100 g) is favourable, but portion control and avoidance of sodium-rich sauces are recommended to limit cumulative cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium may worsen RA-related tissue swelling; calcium content supports bone protection
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lobster ball provides calcium (106 mg per 100 g) beneficial for bone protection in rheumatoid arthritis patients, but the high sodium content (535 mg per 100 g) may exacerbate fluid retention and joint swelling; portion size should be limited and sodium-rich dipping sauces avoided.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lobster Ball is a processed seafood product popular in Singaporean and broader Southeast Asian cuisine, particularly in Malay culinary contexts. It is made by mincing lobster meat, combining it with starch binders such as cornstarch or tapioca flour, salt, and seasoning, then shaping the mixture into compact spherical balls that are boiled, steamed, or added to hotpot broths and soup noodle dishes. The texture is firm and springy, characteristic of Asian fishball-style products, with a mild but distinctly oceanic lobster flavour. Per 100 g, lobster ball provides 97 kcal, 20.91 g protein, 0.95 g fat, and negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g), making it a high-protein, low-calorie, low-fat seafood option. Sodium is elevated at 535 mg per 100 g, reflecting the processed nature of the product. Cholesterol is notable at 161 mg per 100 g, consistent with crustacean-derived foods. Calcium is relatively high at 106 mg per 100 g. The glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. Clinically, lobster ball is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its high protein and negligible carbohydrate profile, but requires caution for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and cholesterol content. It is commonly served in hotpot, laksa, mee pok, and other noodle soups across Singapore hawker centres and food courts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lobster ball?
Is lobster ball suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat lobster ball if I have high blood pressure?
What is lobster ball made of?
Is lobster ball high in protein?
How much sodium is in lobster ball?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Lobster Ball (cr_id G00010749), an approved primary regional source, with input confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15147), an approved secondary global source, and documented in notes. GI not-applicable determination is grounded in the University of Sydney GI Database methodology and the peer-reviewed International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care).
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database (G00010749); lobster meat contains negligible carbohydrates and the starch binder quantity is insufficient to register above 0.0 g at the reported precision. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo — lobster ball contains no plant-derived fibre. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 as crustacean-derived seafood products do not contain industrially produced trans fats. Saturated_fat_g (0.21 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.16 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.27 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for spiny lobster (NDB 15147) proportionally scaled to the reported total fat of 0.95 g per 100 g; HPB does not separately report fatty acid sub-fractions for this item. Fields set to -1 (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g): detailed vitamin, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific processed lobster ball product are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for G00010749, and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15147) does not provide an exact match for this processed Singaporean seafood ball formulation; these fields are set to -1 to indicate data not available from approved sources. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g per 100 g per HPB Foodo); this determination is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classification practice and the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). GL per serving is 0.0 by definition.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Lobster Ball', a precise canonical English name. All five mandatory Singaporean language names are present: English (food_name), Chinese simplified (龙虾丸), Chinese traditional (龍蝦丸), Malay (Bebola Udang Karang), Tamil (லாப்ஸ்டர் உருண்டை), Hindi (लॉबस्टर बॉल).

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (97.0), protein_g (20.91), fat_g (0.95), carbohydrate_g (0.0) all present and numeric. Primary source is HPB Singapore Foodo (approved primary regional source, exact match, cr_id G00010749). Secondary source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source) for fatty acid sub-fractions. Zero carbohydrate justified in notes as confirmed zero per HPB Foodo. nutrient_source clearly states both approved sources and the adaptation basis.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (535.0), saturated_fat_g (0.21), sugar_g (0.0) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible and supported by HPB primary source and USDA fatty acid estimation.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) with gi_category 'not_applicable'. gi_source now cites the peer-reviewed International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) and University of Sydney GI Database methodology — both are approved GI sources under Project Veritas v2. GL per serving is 0.0. source_match_type is 'analogous_estimate' with full documentation. Compliant with the zero-carbohydrate GI rule and g2 approved-source requirement.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided with closest_parent match to seafood ball food product class with gap note. FoodEx2 A0CKP used as closest_parent defensible processed crustacean product code with gap note. SNOMED CT null with honest gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, preparation method, texture, flavour, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrient profile. Suitable for RAG and LLM training.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium-focused guidance), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, negligible carbohydrate and high protein guidance), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, cholesterol and sodium guidance). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases flat array contains 11 entries covering English variants, Chinese script forms, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized form. aliases_by_language is populated for all five Singaporean mandatory languages. Self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified verbatim in flat aliases array — Lobster Ball ✓, Lobster Fishball ✓, Seafood Lobster Ball ✓, Lobster Surimi Ball ✓, Lobster Meat Ball ✓, 龙虾丸 ✓, 龍蝦丸 ✓, Bebola Udang Karang ✓, லாப்ஸ்டர் உருண்டை ✓, लॉबस्टर बॉल ✓, Lóng xiā wán ✓. Consistency check passed.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Lobster Ball (G00010749) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. Iteration 3 repairs: (1) gi_source now cites Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care — a peer-reviewed approved GI source — resolving the g2 approved-source compliance failure; (2) nutrient_source explicitly names both HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as approved sources, resolving the q2 source integrity failure; (3) glycemic_index remains null (not 0) with gi_category 'not_applicable', resolving the q4 numeric requirement. All five mandatory Singaporean language names are present. Zero macros are justified. All -1 sentinel fields are documented. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance.
